dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI won the day: ChatGPT Images 2.0 drew net-positive hands-on feedback and Codex picked up favorable comparison mentions, while Anthropic and Microsoft Copilot absorbed most of the backlash [E1][E7][E5][E4].
winnerExplanation: OpenAI had the strongest positive Hacker News vibe because its highest-visibility story was a direct product launch that commenters treated as a real improvement, even if imperfect [E1]. OpenAI also benefited indirectly from Anthropic frustration, with some commenters saying Codex was working well for them as an alternative [E7]. Google Gemini had positive image-model comparison chatter too, but it was thinner and mostly secondary to OpenAI’s launch thread [E2]. Anthropic had the heaviest negative coverage across multiple stories, and Microsoft Copilot’s single major thread was sharply negative [E3][E5][E6][E4].

openai.judgementSnippet: Mildly positive: the ChatGPT Images 2.0 launch was received as a meaningful improvement despite visible misses, and Anthropic-plan frustration sent some commenters toward Codex as a better-value coding option [E1][E7].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Strongly negative: multiple front-page threads hit Anthropic for policy whiplash, unclear Claude Code / CLI terms, opaque plan testing, and broader skepticism about its economics [E3][E5][E6].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Mildly positive but limited in scope: in image-generation comparisons, commenters often said Google’s Nano Banana / NB2 matched or beat OpenAI on visual fidelity and some hard prompts [E2].

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: Strongly negative: GitHub Copilot’s individual-plan changes were widely framed as a rug pull, with anger over pricier Opus access, removed models, and weak refund/clarity options [E4].

evidence.summary: In the ChatGPT Images 2.0 thread, commenters described OpenAI’s newer image model as a significant quality improvement and said gpt-image-2 edged out prior leaders on one prompt-adherence benchmark, while still noting concrete failures on hard prompts and some output details.

The same image-model thread included favorable comparisons for Google’s Nano Banana / NB2, with commenters saying it matched OpenAI on adherence, had better visual fidelity, and outperformed OpenAI on at least one hard prompt test.

The OpenClaw / Claude CLI story was dominated by complaints that Anthropic’s statements and enforcement were inconsistent: users described contradictory guidance, blocked prompts, unclear subscription boundaries, and unreliable policy communication.

The GitHub Copilot plan-change thread was sharply negative, with commenters calling the changes a rug pull after Opus access became more expensive or unavailable and refund / grandfathering expectations seemed poor.

The Claude Code / Pro-plan thread reacted badly to Anthropic’s apparent test around subscription access, with commenters criticizing opaque messaging, documentation changes, and the damage done to Anthropic’s developer reputation.

The Amazon funding and cloud-spend story triggered skepticism about Anthropic’s economics, with commenters questioning whether proprietary AI labs can justify their costs or avoid commoditization and financing risks.

Inside the Anthropic subscription-backlash thread, commenters favorably mentioned OpenAI’s Codex 5.4, saying it worked well for them at the $20 tier and compared well against Anthropic’s lower-end coding experience.
